source: Invested by Aleph — Semiconductors, AI, global warming, geopolitics
summary: Israeli venture semis and cyber are attractive because founders are experienced operators (age ~50) with proven track records, unlike typical software startups
summary_extended: Baker argues that semiconductor venture investing offers a structurally superior risk-adjusted return profile compared to software venture because the founders are typically experienced operators around age 50 who previously ran divisions at major semiconductor companies and bring their entire teams with them, rather than first-time founders in their mid-to-late twenties. This dramatically reduces execution risk, as these founders have already demonstrated they can build and manage large technical organizations. Baker extends this logic to Israeli cybersecurity startups, which share the same characteristic of experienced, battle-tested founders with proven track records. Atreides has allocated approximately 30-40% of its venture portfolio to Israel, reflecting Baker's high conviction in this structural advantage. The investment implication is a preference for Israeli semiconductor and cyber venture deals over typical software venture, though no specific publicly traded equities are the subject of this thesis.
detail: Baker argues semiconductor venture investing is fundamentally different from software venture because you're often backing a 50-year-old who ran a division at a major company and brought their team. Israeli cyber shares this characteristic. This reduces execution risk vs. backing 25-30 year old first-time founders. Atreides has ~30-40% of its venture portfolio in Israel.
confidence_evidence: Baker explicitly calls this out as a capital deployment priority and explains the structural reason for the risk-adjusted advantage
quote_fragment: in semiconductors often you're investing in a 50-year-old who ran a division
